Achalasia and Risk of Esophageal Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma in a Multinational Study
  • Source: Gastroenterology, Vol. XXX, 2026 (Accepted: 23 March 2026)
  • Topic: Association between achalasia and esophageal cancer subtypes
  • Author(s): Leijonmarck W, Santoni G, Holmberg D, Spechler S, von Euler-Chelpin M, Birgisson H, Ness-Jensen E, Kauppila JH, Lagergren J
Study Overview
  • Population-based case-control study across five Nordic countries
  • Countries --> Denmark, Finland, Iceland, Norway, Sweden
  • Study period --> 1987–2023 (varied by country)
  • Data sources --> National health registries (NordGETS database)
Study Population
  • Cases: n=35,604 esophageal cancer patients
  • Excluded --> 760 (2.0%) due to:
  • <1 year patient registry history
  • Cancer diagnosed within 1 year of achalasia diagnosis (pseudo-achalasia)
  • 96 squamous cell carcinomas with achalasia
  • 41 adenocarcinomas with achalasia
  • Controls: n=355,869 (10:1 matched to cases)
  • Excluded --> 23 (0.006%) due to esophagectomy before index date
Matching Criteria
  • Age at index date
  • Sex
  • Calendar year at index date
  • Country of residence
Key Results Summary
Overall Esophageal Cancer Risk
  • Achalasia present in 0.38% of cases vs 0.06% of controls
  • OR --> 5.73 (95% CI 4.61–7.12) in main model
  • OR --> 3.46 (95% CI 2.76–4.34) after GERD adjustment
Squamous Cell Carcinoma Risk
  • Strong association --> OR 9.46 (95% CI 7.07–12.65)
  • With myotomy/dilation --> OR 16.33 (95% CI 11.26–23.67)
  • After GERD adjustment --> OR 7.46 (95% CI 5.55–10.02)
Adenocarcinoma Risk
  • Moderate association --> OR 3.05 (95% CI 2.14–4.35)
  • After GERD adjustment --> OR 1.44 (95% CI 0.99–2.09) [non-significant]
  • With myotomy/dilation + GERD adjustment --> OR 1.32 (95% CI 0.76–2.30)
Study Demographics
Age Distribution
  • Median age --> 70 years (IQR 62–77)
  • Same for cases and controls
Sex Distribution
  • Male --> 70.9% (252,400 controls; 25,254 cases)
  • Female --> 29.1% (103,469 controls; 10,350 cases)
Country Distribution
  • Sweden --> 39.8% (141,481 controls; 14,159 cases)
  • Denmark --> 33.4% (118,704 controls; 11,871 cases)
  • Finland --> 18.6% (66,055 controls; 6,611 cases)
  • Norway --> 7.3% (26,129 controls; 2,613 cases)
  • Iceland --> 1.0% (3,500 controls; 350 cases)
Risk Factor Prevalence
  • Smoking-related diseases --> 10.2% in cases vs 4.8% in controls
  • Alcohol-related diseases --> 9.2% in cases vs 2.5% in controls
  • GERD --> 14.7% in cases vs 4.8% in controls
Statistical Models Used
Model 1: Unadjusted
  • Matched for age, sex, calendar year, country only
Model 2: Main Multivariable
  • Matched for age, sex, calendar year, country
  • Adjusted for smoking-related diseases
  • Adjusted for alcohol-related diseases
Model 3: Mechanistic (for Adenocarcinoma)
  • All above adjustments
  • Additional adjustment for GERD
Stratified Analyses Results
Age Group Effects
  • 18–64 years --> OR 8.81 (95% CI 5.69–13.67)
  • 65–74 years --> OR 5.69 (95% CI 3.88–8.37)
  • ≥75 years --> OR 4.54 (95% CI 3.24–6.35)
Sex Differences
  • Male --> OR 5.07 (95% CI 3.93–6.53)
  • Female --> OR 8.23 (95% CI 5.37–12.60)
Calendar Period Effects
  • 1987–2002 --> OR 6.36 (95% CI 3.71–10.89)
  • 2003–2013 --> OR 5.62 (95% CI 3.92–8.06)
  • ≥2014 --> OR 5.61 (95% CI 4.09–7.69)
Country-Specific Results
  • Denmark --> OR 6.22 (95% CI 4.48–8.64)
  • Finland --> OR 5.43 (95% CI 3.07–9.61)
  • Iceland --> OR 10.00 (95% CI 0.63–159.88) [wide CI]
  • Norway --> OR 2.34 (95% CI 0.82–6.64)
  • Sweden --> OR 5.98 (95% CI 4.17–8.59)
Smoking Effects
  • With smoking-related diseases --> OR 2.80 (95% CI 1.49–5.29)
  • Without smoking-related diseases --> OR 6.32 (95% CI 5.03–7.95)
Alcohol Effects
  • With alcohol-related diseases --> OR 2.64 (95% CI 1.19–5.86)
  • Without alcohol-related diseases --> OR 6.07 (95% CI 4.86–7.58)
Detection Bias Analysis
  • All-cause mortality compared between cases with vs without achalasia
  • Crude HR --> 1.05 (95% CI 0.88–1.26)
  • Adjusted HR --> 1.09 (95% CI 0.91–1.31)
  • Result --> No significant difference found
Proposed Mechanisms
Squamous Cell Carcinoma Pathway
  • Stagnant food/fluid retention in esophagus
  • Bacterial overgrowth from decomposition
  • Chemical irritation of esophageal mucosa
  • Progression --> chronic non-peptic esophagitis --> mucosal hyperproliferation --> dysplasia --> squamous cell carcinoma
Adenocarcinoma Pathway
  • GERD as primary mediator (attenuated after adjustment)
  • Possible sources of reflux:
  • Iatrogenic reflux post-myotomy or pneumatic dilation
  • Impaired esophageal clearance allowing acid persistence
  • Lactic acid from fermentation of stagnant food
Clinical Implications
Surveillance Recommendations
  • Routine cancer surveillance endoscopy not recommended
  • Low absolute risk despite high relative risk
  • Cost-effectiveness unclear
Clinical Action
  • Prompt endoscopy for suspicious symptoms:
  • Worsening dysphagia
  • Involuntary weight loss
Study Limitations Noted
  • Possible misclassification in registries for:
  • Achalasia diagnosis
  • Smoking-related diseases
  • Alcohol-related diseases
  • GERD
  • Residual confounding possible:
  • Obesity not adjusted (may act as mediator)
  • Smoking/alcohol diagnoses may miss lighter users
  • No adjustment for:
  • Obesity (not associated with SCC but is risk factor for adenocarcinoma)
Strengths of This Study
Size and Power
  • 137 achalasia patients with esophageal cancer (vs previous studies: 3–24)
  • Extended follow-up period across five countries
  • Good statistical precision for subgroup analyses
Data Quality
  • Prospectively collected data from nationwide registries
  • High completeness (>90% sensitivity for surgical procedures)
  • Histology-specific risk estimates
Methodology
  • Population-based design enhances generalizability
  • Three statistical models for different adjustment levels
  • Detection bias analysis included
Key Definitions Used in Study
Achalasia Diagnosis Codes
  • ICD-10 --> K22.0
  • Sweden ICD-9 --> 530A
  • Finland ICD-9 --> 5300A
Intervention Codes (Myotomy/Dilation)
  • NOMESCO codes --> JCE20, JCA21, JCA55
  • Swedish classification of operations --> 2802, 2860, 2861
Smoking-Related Disease Codes
  • ICD-10 --> Z72.0, F17.2, J40-J44, J47, Z71.6
  • COPD and tobacco use disorders
Alcohol-Related Disease Codes
  • ICD-10 --> F10, K70, G31.2, I42.6
  • Includes liver disease, cardiomyopathy, polyneuropathy
GERD Codes
  • ICD-10 --> K20, K21, K22.7, K44, R12
Median Time to Cancer Development
  • Between achalasia diagnosis and esophageal cancer
  • 10 years (interquartile range 5–21)
Funding and Disclosures
  • Grant support --> Swedish Cancer Society (24 3414)
  • Disclosures --> None reported
Registry Coverage by Country
Country Study Period
Denmark 1987–2022
Finland 1987–2018
Iceland 1999–2019
Norway 2008–2019
Sweden 1987–2023
Main Conclusions
For Squamous Cell Carcinoma
  • Achalasia is strongly and independently associated
  • Risk persists after adjustment for smoking, alcohol, GERD
  • Dose-response pattern with treatment intervention
For Adenocarcinoma
  • Association is moderate and largely explained by GERD
  • Becomes non-significant after GERD adjustment
  • Likely mediated through reflux mechanisms
Overall Clinical Message
  • Low proportion (<1%) of achalasia in esophageal cancer patients
  • Does not support routine surveillance endoscopy
  • High clinical suspicion needed for cancer symptoms in achalasia patients
